params.text:
Principles for common tasks

The moves you do most often are the ones most likely to catch you out. The principles below apply to all of them — but reading about a task is never a substitute for being shown it and practising under a trainer before you do it for real.

  • Plan first: read the person’s handling plan, clear the space, and check any equipment.
  • Encourage independence: let the person do what they safely can; you assist, you do not take over.
  • Keep close and stable: work near your body, keep a stable base and move your feet instead of twisting.
  • Communicate: explain what will happen and agree a clear signal so you move together.

Three tasks you will meet often

These are the everyday moves where technique and equipment matter most.

  • Sit-to-stand: the person shuffles to the edge of the seat, leans forward ‘nose over toes’ and rises using the chair arms; you guide and steady rather than pull them up.
  • Repositioning in bed: use slide sheets and, where needed, a profiling bed to move someone up or turn them, so you never drag them by hand.
  • Bed to chair: for someone who can weight-bear, a stand aid or a well-planned assisted transfer; for someone who cannot, a hoist.

If the plan calls for a hoist, use a hoist. Choosing a quicker manual method to save time is exactly how both you and the person get hurt.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on everyday moving tasks.
params.textField: To help someone stand, ask them to shuffle forward and lean ‘nose over *toes*’ before rising. To move a person up or turn them in bed, use *slide* sheets so you never drag them. Where the handling plan calls for a *hoist*, you must use one rather than a quicker manual method. Throughout any task you should encourage the person’s *independence* and let them do what they safely can. Always move your *feet* rather than twisting your spine.
